The default action target is <target>newTabInCurrentPane</target>, so the common pattern is to open a new terminal tab in the current pane and start Codex, Claude Code, or OpenCode there.
customCommands.customActionsDesc: An <actions>actions</actions> entry is the reusable thing cmux runs. Use actions when the same behavior should be available from the Command Palette, surface tab bar, shortcuts, or the plus-button menu. Keep <commands>commands</commands> for reusable shell commands and workspace layouts. Set <palette>palette</palette> to false when an action should stay out of Command Palette.
customCommands.actionTypeBuiltin: Alias a built-in cmux action such as cmux.newTerminal, cmux.newBrowser, cmux.splitRight, or cmux.splitDown.
customCommands.actionTypeCommand: Run shell text in a terminal. Use target to choose the current terminal or a new tab in the current pane.
customCommands.actionTypeAgent: Start a coding agent in a new terminal tab. Any CLI name works (claude, codex, opencode, or your own binary), with optional args.
customCommands.actionTypeWorkspaceCommand: Run a named workspace definition from commands. Use this for multi-pane layouts, custom working directories, and startup commands.
customCommands.actionTypeWorkspace: Create a workspace from an inline workspace definition, with optional restart behavior. Offered in the new-workspace plus-button menu automatically.
customCommands.actionFieldTitle: Command Palette row title, surface button label, menu item title, and trust prompt title unless an entry overrides it.
customCommands.actionFieldSubtitle: Command Palette secondary text. description is accepted as an alias for subtitle.
customCommands.actionFieldKeywords: Extra search terms for Command Palette.
customCommands.actionFieldPalette: Defaults to true for custom actions. Set false to hide the action from Command Palette while keeping it callable elsewhere.
customCommands.actionFieldShortcut: Optional action shortcut, using the same single-stroke or two-stroke chord syntax as settings shortcuts.
customCommands.actionFieldTarget: For command and agent actions only. Use currentTerminal or newTabInCurrentPane.
customCommands.actionFieldNewWorkspaceMenu: Set true to offer any action in the new-workspace plus-button menu, or false to hide a workspace action from it.
customCommands.commandPaletteBehaviorDesc: Command Palette reads the resolved action registry. Custom action IDs are added as rows when <palette>palette</palette> is not false. Legacy <commands>commands</commands> are added automatically as custom rows unless an action with the same generated ID already exists. Built-in commands keep their normal palette labels, but overriding a built-in ID such as <newTerminal>cmux.newTerminal</newTerminal> changes the behavior behind that shared entrypoint.
customCommands.commandPaletteScreenshotAlt: cmux Command Palette showing a custom Codex Worktree action from cmux.json
customCommands.commandPaletteScreenshotCaption: Custom actions with palette enabled show up as searchable Command Palette rows.
customCommands.plusButtonScreenshotAlt: cmux plus button right-click menu showing Codex Worktree, New Terminal, and New Browser
customCommands.plusButtonScreenshotCaption: The right-click menu follows the order from ui.newWorkspace.contextMenu.
customCommands.newWorkspaceButtonDesc: Use <action>ui.newWorkspace.action</action> to override what the plus button does. Use <contextMenu>ui.newWorkspace.contextMenu</contextMenu> (or the <rightClick>rightClick</rightClick> alias) to define the ordered right-click menu. Menu entries can be action IDs, action objects, or <separator>{ "type": "separator" }</separator>.
customCommands.newWorkspaceWorktreeNote: This example makes the normal plus click run the <action>worktree-agents</action> action. The workspace command from <commands>commands</commands> uses a visible setup terminal to create the Git <worktree>worktree</worktree> first. Codex and Claude start at the same time, wait for the workspace-specific state file, then cd into the created directory before exec.
customCommands.workspaceActionsDesc: A workspace layout is stored as a <workspace>workspace</workspace> action, embedding a full workspace definition directly in the action so it needs no separate <commands>commands</commands> entry. It supports the same workspace fields plus <setup>setup</setup>, a bootstrap command sent to the first terminal before that terminal's own command:
customCommands.workspaceActionsMenuDesc: Workspace layouts appear in the plus-button right-click menu automatically. Set <newWorkspaceMenu>newWorkspaceMenu</newWorkspaceMenu> to <falseValue>false</falseValue> to hide one, or to <trueValue>true</trueValue> on any other action to add it to the menu.
customCommands.workspaceActionsSaveDesc: You can also create these without writing JSON: right-click the plus button and choose <saveLayout>Save Workspace as Layout</saveLayout> to capture the current workspace's splits, directories, running agents, and browser tabs into <configPath>~/.config/cmux/cmux.json</configPath>. They are stored as entries in the <actions>actions</actions> block. <customize>Customize Workspace Layouts</customize> opens that file in your editor.
customCommands.simpleCommandsDesc: A simple command runs a shell command in the currently focused terminal:
customCommands.fieldKeywords: Extra search terms for the command palette
customCommands.fieldCommand: Shell command to run in the focused terminal
customCommands.fieldConfirm: Show a confirmation dialog before running
customCommands.simpleCommandCwdNote: Simple commands run in the focused terminal's current working directory. If your command relies on project-relative paths, prefix it with
customCommands.simpleCommandCwdRepoRoot: to run from the repo root, or
customCommands.workspaceCommandsDesc: A workspace command creates a new workspace with a custom layout of splits, terminals, and browser panes:
customCommands.wsFieldName: Workspace tab name (defaults to command name)
customCommands.wsFieldEnv: Environment variables inherited by every shell in the workspace
customCommands.wsFieldSetup: Bootstrap command sent to the workspace's first terminal before that terminal's own command
customCommands.wsFieldLayout: Layout tree defining splits and panes
customCommands.restartBehaviorDesc: Controls what happens when a workspace with the same name already exists:
customCommands.layoutTreeDesc: The layout tree defines how panes are arranged using recursive split nodes:
customCommands.splitChildren: Exactly two child nodes (split or pane)
customCommands.paneNodeDesc: A leaf node containing one or more surfaces (tabs within the pane).
customCommands.surfaceDefinitionDesc: Each surface in a pane can be a terminal or a browser:
customCommands.surfaceCommand: Shell command to auto-run on creation (terminal only)
customCommands.workspaceActionsDefaultDesc: Use the <defaultMenu>Default for New Workspace</defaultMenu> submenu to pick which saved workspace layout plain New Workspace should run, or choose None to return to a blank terminal. The save dialog also has a <checkbox>Use as default for new workspaces</checkbox> checkbox. The setting is <action>ui.newWorkspace.action</action>; a project-local <localConfig>.cmux/cmux.json</localConfig> value wins over <globalConfig>~/.config/cmux/cmux.json</globalConfig>.

keyboardShortcuts.title: Keyboard Shortcuts
keyboardShortcuts.description: Default cmux keyboard shortcuts. Every cmux-owned shortcut can be changed in Settings or ~/.config/cmux/cmux.json, including two-step chords.
keyboardShortcuts.metaDescription: Default cmux keyboard shortcuts for workspaces, surfaces, split panes, browser, notifications, find, and app controls on macOS. All cmux-owned shortcuts are configurable in Settings or cmux.json.
keyboardShortcuts.chordsIntro: cmux supports two-step shortcut chords in <settingsFile>~/.config/cmux/cmux.json</settingsFile>. For the full configuration schema, see the <configurationLink>configuration docs</configurationLink>.
keyboardShortcuts.chordsCallout: Settings can edit shortcuts directly. Use cmux.json when you want an exact tmux-style prefix binding, keep shortcuts in dotfiles, or unbind an action with null, an empty string, "none", "clear", "unbound", or "disabled".
keyboardShortcuts.chordsRuleSingle: Use a plain string for a one-step shortcut.
keyboardShortcuts.chordsRuleArray: Use a two-item array for a chord. The first item is the prefix stroke, the second is the key that follows it.
keyboardShortcuts.chordsRuleSyntax: Each item uses the same syntax as regular bindings, for example cmd+b, ctrl+b, shift+/, or ctrl+1.

cat.appBlurb: Application, window, and top-level command shortcuts.
cat.workspacesBlurb: Workspaces live in the sidebar. Each workspace has its own set of panes and surfaces.
cat.surfacesBlurb: Surfaces are tabs inside a pane.
cat.canvasBlurb: A freeform 2D layout mode where panes float on an infinite, pannable canvas. Alignment and distribution commands are available from the command palette.

api.title: CLI Reference
api.metaDescription: cmux CLI and Unix socket API reference. Workspace management, split panes, input control, notifications, sidebar metadata, environment variables, and detection methods.
api.intro: cmux provides both a CLI tool and a Unix socket for programmatic control. Every command is available through both interfaces.
api.socketOverride: Override with the CMUX_SOCKET_PATH environment variable. Send one newline-terminated JSON request per call:
api.socketCallout: JSON socket requests must use method and params. Legacy v1 JSON payloads such as <legacy>'{'"command":"..."'}'</legacy> are not supported.
api.cmuxOnlyMode: Only processes spawned inside cmux terminals can connect.
api.allowAllMode: Allow any local process to connect (no ancestry check).
api.accessCallout: On shared machines, use Off or cmux processes only.
api.idFormat: Control identifier format in JSON output
api.newSplitDesc: Create a new split pane. Directions: left, right, up, down.
api.listSurfacesDesc: List all surfaces in the current workspace.
api.sendDesc: Send text input to the focused terminal.
api.sendKeyDesc: Send a key press. Keys: enter, tab, escape, backspace, delete, up, down, left, right.
api.sendSurfaceDesc: Send text to a specific surface.
api.sendKeySurfaceDesc: Send a key press to a specific surface.
api.sidebarMetadataDesc: Set status pills, progress bars, and log entries in the sidebar for any workspace. Useful for build scripts, CI integrations, and AI coding agents that want to surface state at a glance.
api.setStatusDesc: Set a sidebar status pill. Use a unique key so different tools can manage their own entries.
api.clearStatusDesc: Remove a sidebar status entry by key.
api.listStatusDesc: List all sidebar status entries for a workspace.
api.setProgressDesc: Set a progress bar in the sidebar (0.0 to 1.0).
api.logDesc: Append a log entry to the sidebar. Levels: info, progress, success, warning, error.
api.sidebarStateDesc: Dump all sidebar metadata (cwd, git branch, ports, status, progress, logs).
api.pingDesc: Check if cmux is running and responsive.
api.capabilitiesDesc: List available socket methods and current access mode.
api.socketPathDesc: Override the socket path used by CLI and integrations
api.socketModeDesc: Override access mode (cmuxOnly, allowAll, off). Also accepts cmux-only/cmux_only and allow-all/allow_all
api.envCallout: Legacy CMUX_SOCKET_MODE values full and notifications are still accepted for compatibility.
api.listPanelsDesc: List all surfaces in the current workspace.
api.listPaneSurfacesDesc: List surfaces in the focused pane.

notifications.title: Notifications
notifications.metaDescription: Send desktop notifications from AI agents and scripts in cmux. CLI, OSC 99/777 escape sequences, and Claude Code hooks integration.
notifications.intro: cmux supports desktop notifications, allowing AI agents and scripts to alert you when they need attention.
notifications.received: Received: notification appears in panel, desktop alert fires (if not suppressed)
notifications.unread: Unread: badge shown on workspace tab
notifications.read: Read: cleared when you view that workspace
notifications.suppressItem2: The specific workspace sending the notification is active
notifications.notificationPanelDesc: Press <openShortcut>⌘⇧I</openShortcut> to open the notification panel. Click a notification to jump to that workspace. Press <jumpShortcut>⌘⇧U</jumpShortcut> to jump directly to the workspace with the most recent unread notification.
notifications.customCommandDesc: Run a shell command every time a notification is scheduled. Set it in Settings > App > Notification Command. The command runs via /bin/sh -c with these environment variables:
notifications.customCommandNote: The command runs independently of the system sound picker. Set the picker to "None" to use only the custom command, or keep both for a system sound plus a custom action.
notifications.osc777Desc: The RXVT protocol uses a fixed format with title and body:
notifications.osc99Desc: The Kitty protocol supports subtitles and notification IDs:
notifications.envTitle: Notification title (workspace name or app name)
notifications.comparisonCallout: Use OSC 777 for simple notifications. Use OSC 99 when you need subtitles or notification IDs. Use the CLI (cmux notify) for the easiest integration.
notifications.claudeCodeHooksDesc: cmux integrates with <link>Claude Code</link> via hooks to notify you when tasks complete.
notifications.restartNote: Restart Claude Code to apply the hooks.
notifications.copilotCliHooksDesc: Copilot CLI supports <link>hooks</link> that run shell commands at lifecycle events like prompt submission, agent stop, and errors.
notifications.copilotCliRepoHooks: For repo-level hooks, create a .github/hooks/notify.json file with the same structure:
notifications.tmuxDesc: If using tmux inside cmux, enable passthrough:
notifications.hooksIntro: <config>cmux.json</config> can define notification hooks that receive every notification policy as JSON on stdin. Each hook returns JSON on stdout. Hooks are off by default; cmux only runs them when <hooksMode>notifications.hooks</hooksMode> contains at least one enabled hook. cmux applies the returned notification text and effects, so hooks can filter banners, keep or skip sidebar history, run sounds, or stop later hooks.
notifications.hooksDetails: Hooks are inherited from global <globalConfig>~/.config/cmux/cmux.json</globalConfig> and project <projectConfig>.cmux/cmux.json</projectConfig> files from parent directories to the current workspace. Project hooks use the same trust prompt as other project <config>cmux.json</config> commands before they run. Feed approval banners also pass through these hooks; disabling <desktop>desktop</desktop> suppresses the native banner while keeping the Feed item available in cmux. Set <hooksMode>notifications.hooksMode</hooksMode> to <replace>replace</replace> in a project config to ignore inherited hooks. If a hook fails, times out, or returns invalid JSON, cmux uses the default notification behavior and posts a hook failure alert.

browserAutomation.title: Browser Automation
browserAutomation.metaDescription: cmux browser command reference for navigation, DOM interaction, waiting, inspection, JavaScript evaluation, tabs, dialogs, frames, downloads, and browser state.
browserAutomation.intro: The cmux browser command group provides browser automation against cmux browser surfaces. Use it to navigate, interact with DOM elements, inspect page state, evaluate JavaScript, and manage browser session data.
browserAutomation.targetingDesc: Most subcommands require a target surface. You can pass it positionally or with --surface.
browserAutomation.waitingDesc: Use wait to block until selectors, text, URL fragments, load state, or a JavaScript condition is satisfied.
browserAutomation.domDesc: Mutating actions support --snapshot-after for fast verification in scripts.
browserAutomation.inspectionDesc: Use structured getters for scripts and snapshots/screenshots for human review.
browserAutomation.stateDesc: Session data commands cover cookies, local/session storage, and full browser state snapshots.
browserAutomation.tabsDesc: Browser tab operations map to browser surfaces in the active browser tab group.
browserAutomation.patternForm: Fill a form and verify success text

skills.title: Skills
skills.metaDescription: Install and use cmux agent skills for core CLI control, workspace automation, settings, customization, diagnostics, browser automation, and markdown panels.
skills.intro: cmux ships skills that teach coding agents how to use cmux CLI control, current-workspace automation, settings, customization, diagnostics, browser surfaces, and markdown panels.
skills.installIntro: Use Vercel's skills CLI for the standard multi-agent install flow, or use skills.sh when you only need the Codex skills directory.
skills.installDestination: By default, skills.sh installs into ~/.codex/skills, or $CODEX_HOME/skills when CODEX_HOME is set. Pass --dest when your agent reads skills from another directory.
skills.localInstallIntro: From a cloned cmux checkout, the script uses the local skills directory.
skills.pinRefIntro: Use --ref to install from a specific branch, tag, or commit when the script downloads from GitHub.
skills.includedIntro: Each skill has a SKILL.md file, optional references, and an OpenAI metadata file under agents/openai.yaml.
skills.cmuxDescription: Controls windows, workspaces, panes, surfaces, focus, moves, reorder, and routing with the cmux CLI.
skills.cmuxUse: Use it when an agent needs deterministic placement or navigation in a multi-pane cmux layout.
skills.workspaceDescription: Keeps automation scoped to the current caller workspace, caller surface, panes, sidebar metadata, and socket context.
skills.workspaceUse: Use it when an agent should add panes, send input, inspect state, or open helper surfaces without disrupting another workspace.
skills.settingsDescription: Inspects, edits, validates, and opens ~/.config/cmux/cmux.json with a bundled helper script.
skills.settingsUse: Use it when changing appearance, sidebar, notification, browser, automation, or shortcut settings by JSON path.
skills.customizationDescription: Customizes cmux.json actions, plus-button behavior, tab bar buttons, workspace layouts, Dock controls, Feed hooks, sidebar settings, Command Palette entries, shortcuts, and Ghostty-owned terminal preferences.
skills.customizationUse: Use it to make cmux open a user's worktrees, multiple checkouts, SSH sessions, dev tools, or project layout from the exact UI entrypoints they want.
skills.diagnosticsDescription: Runs support-safe checks for cmux CLI health, socket access, hooks, session restore, settings, and agent binaries.
skills.diagnosticsUse: Use it when notifications, hooks, restore, or automation are not behaving as expected.
skills.browserDescription: Automates cmux webview surfaces with snapshot refs, DOM actions, waits, screenshots, and session state.
skills.browserUse: Use it for browser tasks that should run inside cmux instead of a separate browser automation tool.
skills.markdownDescription: Opens markdown files in a formatted cmux panel with live reload.
skills.markdownUse: Use it to show plans, docs, notes, and task lists beside the terminal while work is happening.
skills.coverageIntro: The top-level SKILL.md files stay short. Deeper command details live in references, scripts, and templates next to each skill.
skills.cmuxScope: Topology control for windows, workspaces, panes, surfaces, focus, moves, reorders, split-off flows, and attention flashes.
skills.cmuxReferences: Handle syntax, caller targeting, window and workspace lifecycle, pane and surface routing, trigger flash, and health checks.
skills.workspaceScope: Current-workspace automation rules for caller context, additive panes, helper surfaces, sidebar metadata, input, and socket selection.
skills.workspaceReferences: Workspace command reference covering context, windows, workspaces, panes, surfaces, input, sidebar state, notifications, docs, and tagged reloads.
skills.settingsScope: cmux.json settings reads and writes, key lookup, JSONC parsing, safe atomic updates, validation, editor opening, and shortcut binding edits.
skills.settingsReferences: Generated settings key list, shortcut action ids, schema URL, supported path detection, and the bundled cmux-settings helper.
skills.customizationScope: End-user customization across cmux.json settings, actions, commands, workspace layouts, plus-button click and right-click menus, surface tab bar buttons, Dock controls, Feed and notification hooks, sidebar metadata, Command Palette entries, shortcuts, and Ghostty config boundaries.
skills.customizationReferences: Config-surface selection, what can be customized, global versus project-local scope, action examples, plus-button wiring, tab bar button examples, Dock examples, Feed hooks, workspace layout examples, reload steps, validation rules, and safety constraints.
skills.customizationExamplesIntro: These are reusable workflow patterns that a user can ask an agent to apply with cmux Customization.
skills.exampleWorktreeSurface: Plus-button click, plus-button right-click menu, Command Palette, workspace layout
skills.exampleWorktreeUse: Opening a dedicated worktree or checkout with paired Codex, Claude, SSH, or helper terminals.
skills.exampleFullStackSurface: Workspace command, browser preview, Dock controls
skills.exampleFullStackUse: Starting frontend, backend, tests, logs, and preview panes in the same repeatable layout.
skills.exampleSshSurface: Workspace command, remote terminal, browser surface
skills.exampleSshUse: Connecting to a remote environment while keeping local previews, notes, and navigation in cmux.
skills.exampleReviewSurface: Workspace command, browser surface, markdown or terminal notes
skills.exampleReviewUse: Opening GitHub status, the pull request, and review notes in a single workspace.
skills.exampleDocsSurface: Workspace command, markdown panel, browser preview
skills.exampleDocsUse: Editing documentation with the rendered page, local dev server, and source notes visible.
skills.exampleCiSurface: Dock controls, Feed TUI, notification hooks
skills.exampleCiUse: Watching GitHub Actions, CircleCI, release monitors, and agent hook events without losing the main workspace.
skills.exampleAgentButtonsSurface: Surface tab bar buttons, actions, Command Palette entries
skills.exampleAgentButtonsUse: Adding one-click Codex, Claude, or custom-agent launchers while keeping built-in tab buttons visible.
skills.customizationExamplesCallout: Detailed snippets live in skills/cmux-customization/references/examples.md. Agents load that file only when an examples-library pattern is needed.
skills.customizationPromptWorktree: Use $cmux-customization to set up the worktree-agents example.
skills.customizationPromptFullStack: Use $cmux-customization to create a project-local full-stack-dev layout with Dock controls.
skills.customizationPromptAgentButtons: Use $cmux-customization to add quick-agent-buttons for Codex and Claude.
skills.diagnosticsScope: Read-only health checks for CLI reachability, socket access, cmux environment, settings validation, hook installation markers, session stores, auto-resume settings, and supported agent binaries.
skills.diagnosticsReferences: Bundled support-safe diagnostic script, hook setup commands, session restore interpretation, notification checks, and rules for redacting sensitive files.
skills.browserScope: Browser automation inside cmux webview surfaces, including navigation, DOM actions, waits, state capture, screenshots, and snapshots.
skills.browserReferences: Command mapping, snapshot ref lifecycle, authentication, session persistence, video status, proxy behavior, and reusable automation templates.
skills.markdownScope: Formatted markdown panels that live beside terminals and reload as files change.
skills.markdownReferences: Command syntax, routing options, live reload behavior, atomic file replacement, and markdown rendering coverage.
skills.helpMenuIntro: The macOS <help>Help</help> menu mirrors this docs sidebar and includes <skills>Skills</skills>. Use the Skills item in Help to open this page from the app.
skills.authoringIntro: Keep the main SKILL.md concise. Put deeper command tables, scripts, and reusable templates next to the skill.
skills.authoringCallout: When adding a new skill, include agents/openai.yaml so the installer exposes a clear name, short description, and default prompt.
skills.suggestionsIntro: These are good candidates if the skill set grows beyond the current installed list.
skills.suggestSshUse: Drive remote workspaces, SSH URL launches, browser routing through remote networks, and reconnect behavior.
skills.suggestSshWhy: Remote sessions are a major cmux surface with behavior that differs from local terminal workflows.
skills.suggestCloudVmUse: Operate Cloud VM create, attach, exec, SSH endpoint, billing, and provider troubleshooting flows.
skills.suggestCloudVmWhy: Cloud VM work crosses the web app, database, provider APIs, and smoke tests.
skills.suggestVaultUse: Manage vault-backed agent configuration, credentials, and restore behavior without leaking secrets into prompts.
skills.suggestVaultWhy: Vault workflows need stricter handling than normal settings because they affect secrets and agent startup.
skills.suggestionsCallout: Keep future skills focused on repeatable workflows with real command sequences. If a skill would only restate product docs, add it to docs instead.

ssh.title: SSH
ssh.metaDescription: Connect to remote machines with cmux ssh. Browser panes route through the remote network, images drag-and-drop via scp, and agent notifications come home.
ssh.intro: cmux ssh creates a workspace for a remote machine. Browser panes route through the remote network, files drag-and-drop via scp, coding agents send notifications to your local sidebar, and sessions reconnect on drops.
ssh.usageDesc: cmux ssh reads your ~/.ssh/config for host aliases, identity files, and proxy settings. All flags mirror their ssh equivalents.
ssh.flagNoFocus: Create the workspace without switching to it
ssh.deepLinksDesc: Use cmux SSH deep links when a website or tool wants to offer an Open in cmux button. The link opens cmux, shows a confirmation prompt, then runs the equivalent cmux ssh command after the user confirms.
ssh.deepLinksWebFallbackDesc: Use the cmux.com fallback URL for website buttons. It opens the native link and shows the download if cmux is not installed.
ssh.deepLinksPromptRulesDesc: Prompt and rules buttons use the same fallback shape. Commas, colons, and literal plus signs are preserved when they are URL encoded:
ssh.deepLinksIconDesc: Use the SVG icon for dashboard buttons, or the PNG logo when a raster image is required:
ssh.deepLinksButtonDesc: Build the fallback URL with URLSearchParams so titles, host aliases, and user names are encoded safely:
ssh.deepLinkUser: Optional SSH user. cmux combines it with host as user@host.
ssh.deepLinkTitle: Optional workspace title. Use only one of title or name.
ssh.deepLinkConnectTimeout: Optional ConnectTimeout value in seconds, 1 through 600.
ssh.deepLinkServerAliveInterval: Optional ServerAliveInterval value in seconds, 1 through 3600.
ssh.deepLinkHostKeyPolicy: Optional StrictHostKeyChecking policy: accept-new, ask, strict, or yes.
ssh.deepLinkNoFocus: Optional boolean. true creates the workspace without switching to it.
ssh.deepLinksSchemeDesc: Use cmux:// for the stable app, cmux-nightly:// for Nightly, and cmux-dev:// for Debug or tagged dev builds.
ssh.deepLinksSecurityDesc: External links cannot pass identity files, raw ssh options, commands, ProxyCommand, or forwarding rules. Put keys, ProxyJump, HostName, and advanced options in ~/.ssh/config instead. cmux displays the command preview and requires the user to trust the SSH target before connecting.
ssh.browserDesc: Browser panes in a remote workspace route all HTTP and WebSocket traffic through the remote machine's network. Type localhost:3000 and you're looking at the dev server running on the remote box. No -L flags, no manual port forwarding. Each remote workspace gets an isolated cookie store so sessions are scoped per-connection.
ssh.dragDropDesc: Drag an image or file into a remote terminal and cmux uploads it via scp through the existing SSH connection. cmux detects the foreground SSH process by TTY and routes the upload through ControlMaster multiplexing.
ssh.notificationsDesc: Processes on the remote machine can run cmux commands that execute on your local instance. When a coding agent calls cmux notify on the remote box, the notification appears in your local sidebar. The blue ring lights up on the workspace tab. Cmd+Shift+U jumps to it. Notification spam from flaky connections is suppressed with a per-host cooldown.
ssh.agentsDesc: cmux claude-teams and cmux omo both work inside SSH sessions. The Go relay daemon on the remote host handles the same tmux-compat translation that the local Swift CLI does. Teammate agents spawn as native cmux splits on your local machine while computation runs on the remote box.
ssh.reconnectDesc: When the connection drops, cmux reconnects with exponential backoff (3s, 6s, 12s, up to 60s). The remote session persists and cmux reattaches on reconnect, resizing with smallest-screen-wins semantics. Default keepalive options (ServerAliveInterval=20, ServerAliveCountMax=2) are injected unless your config already sets them.
ssh.daemonDesc: On first connect, cmux probes the remote host (uname -s, uname -m) and uploads a versioned cmuxd-remote binary. The binary speaks JSON-RPC over stdio and handles three things:
ssh.daemonProxyHow: SOCKS5 and HTTP CONNECT over the daemon's stdio channel
ssh.daemonRelayHow: Reverse TCP tunnel with HMAC-SHA256 auth so remote processes can call cmux commands locally
ssh.daemonSessionHow: Persists sessions across reconnects, coordinates PTY resize across multiple attachments
ssh.daemonPath: The daemon binary is stored at ~/.cmux/bin/cmuxd-remote/<version>/<os>-<arch>/cmuxd-remote on the remote host and verified against a SHA-256 manifest embedded in the app.

changelog.title: Changelog
changelog.metaDescription: cmux release notes and version history. New features, bug fixes, and changes for the native macOS terminal.

claudeCodeTeams.title: Claude Code Teams
claudeCodeTeams.metaDescription: Run Claude Code with agent teams inside cmux. Teammate agents spawn as native cmux splits instead of tmux panes.
claudeCodeTeams.intro: cmux claude-teams launches Claude Code with agent teams enabled. When Claude spawns teammate agents, they appear as native cmux splits instead of tmux panes, with full sidebar metadata and notifications.
claudeCodeTeams.usageDesc: All arguments after claude-teams are forwarded to Claude Code. The command defaults teammate mode to auto and sets the environment so Claude uses cmux splits.
claudeCodeTeams.howItWorksDesc: cmux claude-teams creates a tmux shim script and configures the environment so Claude Code thinks it's running inside tmux. When Claude issues tmux commands to manage teammate panes, the shim translates them into cmux socket API calls.
claudeCodeTeams.shimStep1: Creates a tmux shim at ~/.cmuxterm/claude-teams-bin/tmux that redirects to cmux __tmux-compat
claudeCodeTeams.shimStep2: Sets TMUX and TMUX_PANE environment variables to simulate a tmux session
claudeCodeTeams.shimStep3: Prepends the shim directory to PATH so Claude finds the shim before real tmux
claudeCodeTeams.shimStep4: Enables CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1 and sets teammate mode to auto
claudeCodeTeams.envTmux: Fake tmux socket path encoding the current cmux workspace and pane
claudeCodeTeams.envTmuxPane: Fake tmux pane identifier mapped to the current cmux pane
claudeCodeTeams.envTeams: Enables Claude Code agent teams feature
claudeCodeTeams.envSocket: Path to the cmux control socket for the shim to connect to
claudeCodeTeams.dirShim: Contains the tmux shim script that translates tmux commands to cmux API calls
claudeCodeTeams.dirStore: Persistent storage for tmux-compat buffers and hooks
claudeCodeTeams.tmuxCommandsDesc: The shim translates these tmux commands into cmux operations:
claudeCodeTeams.mapSendText: sends text to a cmux surface
claudeCodeTeams.mapReadText: reads terminal text from a cmux surface
claudeCodeTeams.mapFocus: focuses a cmux pane or workspace
claudeCodeTeams.mapClose: closes a cmux surface or workspace

ohMyOpenCode.title: oh-my-opencode
ohMyOpenCode.metaDescription: oh-my-opencode (cmux omo) runs the OpenCode CLI with the oh-my-openagent plugin inside cmux. Orchestrate Claude, GPT, Gemini, and Grok as parallel agents, each in a native cmux split.
ohMyOpenCode.intro: cmux omo launches OpenCode with the oh-my-openagent plugin in a cmux-aware environment. oh-my-openagent orchestrates multiple AI models (Claude, GPT, Gemini, Grok) as specialized agents working in parallel. When it spawns agent panes, they become native cmux splits.
ohMyOpenCode.usageDesc: All arguments after omo are forwarded to OpenCode.
ohMyOpenCode.whatYouGetDesc: oh-my-openagent's TmuxSessionManager spawns each background agent in its own pane. With cmux omo, those panes become native cmux splits instead of tmux panes:
ohMyOpenCode.whatYouGet1: Each subagent (Hephaestus, Atlas, Oracle, etc.) gets its own cmux split, visible in the workspace
ohMyOpenCode.whatYouGet2: Auto-layout management: agents are arranged in a grid (main-vertical by default) and resized as agents come and go
ohMyOpenCode.whatYouGet3: Idle agents are automatically cleaned up after 3 consecutive idle polls with no new messages
ohMyOpenCode.whatYouGet4: If the window is too small for a new agent pane, it queues and retries every 2 seconds until space is available
ohMyOpenCode.whatYouGet5: Your main session stays in the primary pane while agents work beside it
ohMyOpenCode.firstRunDesc: On first run, cmux omo automatically sets up everything:
ohMyOpenCode.firstRunStep1: Creates a shadow config at ~/.cmuxterm/omo-config/ with oh-my-opencode registered in the plugin array
ohMyOpenCode.firstRunStep2: Installs the oh-my-opencode npm package using bun or npm if not already present
ohMyOpenCode.firstRunStep3: Symlinks node_modules, package.json, and plugin config from your original ~/.config/opencode/ directory
ohMyOpenCode.firstRunStep4: Enables tmux mode in the oh-my-opencode config (tmux.enabled defaults to false, cmux omo turns it on)
ohMyOpenCode.firstRunSafe: Your original ~/.config/opencode/ config is never modified. Running plain opencode works the same as before.
ohMyOpenCode.howItWorksDesc: Same pattern as cmux claude-teams. A tmux shim intercepts tmux commands from oh-my-openagent's TmuxSessionManager and translates them into cmux API calls.
ohMyOpenCode.shimStep1: Creates a tmux shim at ~/.cmuxterm/omo-bin/tmux that redirects to cmux __tmux-compat
ohMyOpenCode.shimStep2: Sets TMUX and TMUX_PANE to simulate a tmux session
ohMyOpenCode.shimStep3: Enables tmux.enabled in the oh-my-opencode config (required for visual pane spawning)
ohMyOpenCode.shimStep4: Points OPENCODE_CONFIG_DIR at the shadow config
ohMyOpenCode.shimStep5: Prepends the shim directory to PATH and execs into opencode
ohMyOpenCode.dirShadow: Shadow OpenCode config with oh-my-opencode plugin registered and tmux enabled (symlinks to your original config)
ohMyOpenCode.dirStore: Persistent storage for tmux-compat buffers and hooks
ohMyOpenCode.shadowConfigDesc: cmux omo uses a shadow config directory so your original OpenCode setup is unaffected:
ohMyOpenCode.shadowStep1: Copies your ~/.config/opencode/opencode.json with oh-my-opencode added to the plugin array
ohMyOpenCode.shadowStep2: Symlinks node_modules, package.json, and bun.lock from the original directory
ohMyOpenCode.shadowStep3: Writes oh-my-opencode.json with tmux.enabled set to true
ohMyOpenCode.shadowStep4: Sets OPENCODE_CONFIG_DIR to the shadow directory before launching opencode
ohMyOpenCode.envTmux: Fake tmux socket path encoding the current cmux workspace and pane
ohMyOpenCode.envTmuxPane: Fake tmux pane identifier mapped to the current cmux pane
ohMyOpenCode.envConfigDir: Points to the shadow config directory with oh-my-opencode enabled
ohMyOpenCode.envSocket: Path to the cmux control socket for the shim to connect to

ohMyCodex.title: oh-my-codex
ohMyCodex.metaDescription: Run Oh My Codex (OMX) inside cmux with native split pane integration for team mode and agent orchestration.
ohMyCodex.intro: cmux omx launches Oh My Codex (OMX) in a cmux-aware environment. OMX is a multi-agent orchestration layer for OpenAI Codex CLI with 30+ specialized agent roles, workflow skills, and tmux-based parallel team execution. When OMX spawns team panes or HUD displays, they become native cmux splits.
ohMyCodex.usageDesc: All arguments after omx are forwarded to the omx CLI.
ohMyCodex.whatYouGetDesc: OMX's team mode and HUD use tmux for pane management. With cmux omx, those panes become native cmux splits:
ohMyCodex.whatYouGet1: Team worker panes (Codex/Claude sessions) appear as cmux splits in the workspace
ohMyCodex.whatYouGet2: HUD status display shows model, branch, context, and token usage in a split pane
ohMyCodex.whatYouGet3: Auto-layout management arranges agent panes in a main-vertical grid
ohMyCodex.whatYouGet4: Your main session stays in the primary pane while workers operate beside it
ohMyCodex.prerequisitesDesc: OMX requires OpenAI Codex CLI and a working Codex auth setup. Run omx doctor to verify your installation.
ohMyCodex.howItWorksDesc: Same pattern as other cmux agent integrations. A tmux shim intercepts tmux commands from OMX and translates them into cmux API calls.
ohMyCodex.shimStep1: Creates a tmux shim at ~/.cmuxterm/omx-bin/tmux that redirects to cmux __tmux-compat
ohMyCodex.shimStep2: Sets TMUX and TMUX_PANE to simulate a tmux session
ohMyCodex.shimStep3: Prepends the shim directory to PATH
ohMyCodex.shimStep4: Execs into omx with all remaining arguments forwarded
ohMyCodex.dirStore: Persistent storage for tmux-compat buffers and hooks
ohMyCodex.envTmux: Fake tmux socket path encoding the current cmux workspace and pane
ohMyCodex.envTmuxPane: Fake tmux pane identifier mapped to the current cmux pane
ohMyCodex.envSocket: Path to the cmux control socket for the shim to connect to

ohMyPi.title: oh-my-pi
ohMyPi.metaDescription: Connect oh-my-pi (omp) to cmux with hooks for status, notifications, session restore, auto-naming, and Task Manager attribution.
ohMyPi.intro: oh-my-pi is a fork of Pi rewritten as a coding-first agent. Its binary is omp, and cmux integrates with it through the omp hooks extension.
ohMyPi.setupUsageDesc: Install omp, then install the cmux hooks extension. The extension is written to ~/.omp/agent/extensions/cmux-omp-session.ts, and cmux upgrades it in place when the bundled version changes.
ohMyPi.whatYouGetDesc: The hooks extension lets omp report lifecycle and session information back to cmux:
ohMyPi.whatYouGet1: Workspace busy and idle status from omp turn boundaries
ohMyPi.whatYouGet3: Session tracking so closed panes can be resumed or forked from the session index with omp --session SESSION_ID, adding --fork for forks
ohMyPi.whatYouGet4: Workspace auto-naming, with omp available as the summarizer model
ohMyPi.whatYouGet5: Task Manager process attribution for omp processes
ohMyPi.directoriesDesc: cmux reads omp data from the agent directory. PI_CODING_AGENT_DIR or PI_CONFIG_DIR can override that directory.
ohMyPi.dirExtension: cmux hooks extension installed and upgraded by cmux
ohMyPi.dirSessions: omp session files that cmux reads for restore and fork workflows
ohMyPi.envHooksDisabled: Disables the omp hooks extension installed by cmux
ohMyPi.envCmuxBin: Overrides the cmux binary that the hooks extension invokes

ohMyClaudeCode.usageDesc: All arguments after omc are forwarded to the omc CLI.
ohMyClaudeCode.whatYouGetDesc: OMC's team mode uses tmux for pane management. With cmux omc, those panes become native cmux splits:
ohMyClaudeCode.whatYouGet1: Team worker panes (Claude/Codex/Gemini sessions) appear as cmux splits in the workspace
ohMyClaudeCode.whatYouGet2: HUD monitoring display shows live status in a split pane
ohMyClaudeCode.whatYouGet3: Auto-layout management arranges agent panes in a main-vertical grid
ohMyClaudeCode.whatYouGet4: Your main session stays in the primary pane while agents work beside it
ohMyClaudeCode.prerequisitesDesc: OMC requires Claude Code CLI installed and authenticated.
ohMyClaudeCode.howItWorksDesc: Same pattern as cmux claude-teams. A tmux shim intercepts tmux commands from OMC and translates them into cmux API calls.
ohMyClaudeCode.shimStep1: Creates a tmux shim at ~/.cmuxterm/omc-bin/tmux that redirects to cmux __tmux-compat
ohMyClaudeCode.shimStep2: Sets TMUX and TMUX_PANE to simulate a tmux session
ohMyClaudeCode.shimStep3: Prepends the shim directory to PATH
ohMyClaudeCode.shimStep4: Injects NODE_OPTIONS restore module for Claude Code compatibility

dock.metaDescription: Add custom terminal UI controls to the cmux right sidebar with Dock JSON.
dock.title: Dock
dock.intro: Dock lets you add terminal UI controls to the right sidebar. Each control is a command from JSON, rendered in its own Ghostty-backed terminal section. Use it for feeds, logs, queues, git status, dev servers, or any TUI your team wants near every workspace.
dock.configIntro: cmux looks for Dock configuration in this order:
dock.projectConfig: for the current repo, nearest parent project, and nested project directories.
dock.globalConfig: for your personal default Dock or when there is no repo.
dock.precedenceCallout: If a project and global config both exist, the project config wins. Nested project configs apply to their directory tree. If no project config exists, Dock uses the global config. If neither file exists, Dock opens empty.
dock.trustCallout: Project Dock configs can start commands. cmux asks you to trust a project config before launching its controls.
dock.exampleIntro: A Dock file is a JSON object with a controls array. Commit project controls to .cmux/dock.json when you want teammates to share the same Dock. Replace example commands with tools your repo actually has.
dock.fieldId: Stable unique identifier for the control. Keep this short and do not reuse it for another command.
dock.fieldTitle: Label shown in the Dock header.
dock.fieldCommand: Command to run in the Dock terminal. It starts inside your login shell.
dock.fieldCwd: Optional working directory. Relative paths resolve from the project root for project configs, or from your home directory for global configs.
dock.fieldHeight: Optional requested terminal height in points. Controls without a height share the remaining space.
dock.fieldEnv: Optional environment variables passed only to that control.
dock.sharingIntro: Dock is designed to be shared with source control when the commands belong to the repo.
dock.sharingProject: Put repo-specific controls in .cmux/dock.json and commit the file.
dock.sharingGlobal: Put personal controls in ~/.config/cmux/dock.json, especially outside a repo, and keep that file out of shared source control.
dock.sharingSecrets: Do not put secrets in dock.json. Read secrets from your shell, a local env file, or your existing dev tooling.
dock.agentPromptTitle: Ask an agent to set it up
dock.agentPromptIntro: Use this prompt when you want a coding agent to create Dock controls. It tells the agent to run `cmux docs dock`, inspect the project, and ask before guessing.
dock.agentPrompt:
Set up cmux Dock controls for the current context.

First, learn the feature before editing:
1. Run `cmux docs dock` if the cmux CLI is available. If it is not, read https://cmux.com/docs/dock.
2. Inspect the repository or current directory to understand the project type, scripts, package manager, dev servers, logs, task runners, test commands, and any existing TUI tools.
3. If the desired Dock is ambiguous, ask the user what they want monitored or controlled before writing files.

Dock is cmux's right-sidebar terminal control area. A Dock config is JSON with a top-level `controls` array. Each control runs a command in its own Ghostty-backed terminal section using the user's login shell. Controls are useful for project dashboards, git/status views, dev server or build status, test watchers, log tails, queues, local services, or a custom TUI such as `cmux feed tui --opentui` when that feed is useful.

Choose where to write the config:
- In a repository or project directory, create or edit `.cmux/dock.json` so teammates can share it.
- For a personal default outside a repo, create or edit `~/.config/cmux/dock.json`.
- If both exist, project `.cmux/dock.json` is more specific for that project. Nested project configs apply to that directory tree; use the nearest relevant project config instead of writing unrelated controls globally.
- If there is no repo and no clear project root, use the global config only after confirming the user wants a personal Dock.

Schema:
{
  "controls": [
    {
      "id": "short-stable-id",
      "title": "Human label",
      "command": "safe command to run",
      "cwd": "optional/path",
      "height": 220,
      "env": { "NAME": "value" }
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Keep ids stable, lowercase, and unique.
- Use `cwd` for subdirectories; relative paths resolve from the config base.
- Use `height` only when a control needs a fixed amount of vertical space.
- Use `env` only for non-secret values needed by one control.
- Do not put secrets, tokens, or machine-specific private paths in a shared project config.
- Prefer commands that are safe to start repeatedly and make sense in a terminal.
- Do not invent unavailable scripts. Read package files, Makefiles, Procfiles, README docs, config files, and existing tooling first.
- Keep shared project Docks portable for teammates. Put personal or machine-specific controls in the global Dock.

Deliverable:
- Create or update the appropriate dock.json.
- Preserve existing useful controls unless the user asked to replace them.
- Validate that the JSON parses.
- Summarize what each control does and any commands the user should review before trusting the Dock config.

textBox.title: TextBox (Beta)
textBox.metaDescription: Configure the beta cmux TextBox input for new terminal tabs, splits, and workspaces.
textBox.intro: TextBox is cmux's rich terminal input surface for composing prompts before sending them to a terminal.
textBox.betaCallout: TextBox is a beta feature. Its defaults and behavior may change while it is being tested.
textBox.defaultsDesc: The TextBox settings live in Settings > TextBox:
textBox.showSetting: Show TextBox on New Terminals opens new workspaces, terminal tabs, and terminal splits with the TextBox visible while keeping focus in the terminal.
textBox.focusSetting: Focus TextBox on New Terminals puts keyboard focus in the TextBox. Focusing also shows the TextBox.
textBox.maxLinesSetting: TextBox Max Lines caps the TextBox height before its content scrolls.
textBox.configDesc: Set the same defaults in ~/.config/cmux/cmux.json under terminal:
textBox.focusNote: Restored sessions keep their saved TextBox visibility and focus state. These defaults apply only to newly created terminal surfaces.

ios.metaDescription: Set up the cmux iOS companion app: TestFlight access, bring-your-own networking with Tailscale or WireGuard, pairing, notifications, and what data cmux stores.
ios.title: iOS App
ios.intro: The cmux iOS app is a companion for your Mac. Pair your iPhone or iPad with a Mac running cmux and attach to your terminals from your phone, with optional forwarding of terminal notifications.
ios.betaNote: The iOS app is in beta. It ships on TestFlight as cmux BETA.
ios.accessDesc: The app is distributed through TestFlight. Early access is included with <foundersLink>cmux Founders Edition</foundersLink>, alongside other early features.
ios.prereq1: A Mac running cmux and signed in.
ios.prereq2: An iPhone or iPad with the cmux BETA app installed from TestFlight.
ios.prereq3: A network path from your phone to your Mac (see Bring your own networking below).
ios.networkingDesc: cmux does not provide networking out of the box. The terminal stream flows directly between your phone and your Mac, so your phone has to be able to reach your Mac on the network. The simplest way is a private overlay network.
ios.tailscaleDesc: Install <link>Tailscale</link> on both your Mac and your phone and sign in to the same tailnet. Your phone can then reach your Mac by its tailnet address from anywhere, with no port forwarding.
ios.wireguardDesc: If you already run <link>WireGuard</link>, put your Mac and phone on the same network and use the Mac's WireGuard address.
ios.networkingNote: Whichever you choose, the connection is yours. cmux does not proxy or relay terminal traffic between your devices.
ios.pairStep1: On the Mac, open the Mobile Connect window in cmux.
ios.pairStep2: On your phone, open the cmux BETA app and confirm the pairing shown in Mobile Connect.
ios.pairStep3: Once paired, your workspaces and terminals appear on the phone over your network.
ios.pairNote: Pairing is per device. A new device, or a reinstalled app, pairs again.
ios.notificationsDesc: You can opt in to forward terminal notifications to your phone, so a pane that needs attention pushes to your device. When forwarding is on, the notification text (title and body, drawn from terminal output) is sent through cmux servers to Apple's push service to deliver it. Turn on Hide content in the notification settings to send a generic message instead and keep terminal text on your devices. See <link>notifications</link> for how cmux raises them.
ios.dataIntro: cmux is built so your terminal stays between your devices. On cmux servers we store only what is needed to sign you in and connect your devices:
ios.data1: Your account from sign-in (email), to identify you across devices.
ios.data2: A push token for your device, to deliver forwarded terminal notifications.
ios.data3: Pairing and device metadata, to connect your phone to your Mac.
ios.dataNot: Your interactive terminal session, its contents and keystrokes, flows directly between your phone and your Mac over your own network, and is not relayed through or stored on cmux servers. The one exception is forwarded notification text, which transits cmux and Apple's push service to reach your phone, unless you enable Hide content (see Notifications above).
ios.enterpriseDesc: For a self-hosted or air-gapped deployment, contact <link>founders@manaflow.com</link>.

vault.title: Vault
vault.metaDescription: Search old Codex, Claude Code, OpenCode, and Pi sessions in cmux Vault, then reopen a session in the workspace where you need it.
vault.intro: Vault is a right-sidebar pane for finding old AI coding agent sessions by transcript content instead of by terminal history.
vault.indexesDesc: Vault reads local session records from supported agents and makes their transcripts searchable inside cmux.
vault.workflowOpen: Open Vault from the right sidebar.
vault.workflowSearch: Search for a file, branch, issue title, error message, or phrase from the conversation.
vault.workflowDrag: Drag a matching session into the current workspace.
vault.workflowContinue: Resume from the recovered context beside the code or browser pane you are using now.
vault.useDesc: Use Vault when you remember what an agent worked on but not which workspace, date, or shell command started it. It is built for rediscovering prior agent context, not for restoring the current app layout after relaunch.
vault.limitsDesc: Vault can only index session formats that cmux knows how to read on the local machine. It does not search private remote hosts unless those sessions are available to the local cmux installation.
vault.blogCta: Read the <link>Vault feature story</link> for a workflow-oriented overview.

taskManager.openDesc: Run the CLI command from any cmux terminal:

taskManager.workflowOpen: Open Task Manager when cmux feels slow, fans spin up, or an agent appears stuck.
taskManager.workflowSort: Sort or scan for the process using the most CPU or memory.
taskManager.workflowJump: Jump back to the owning workspace or surface.
taskManager.workflowFix: Stop, restart, or split the overloaded process with the right context visible.
taskManager.useDesc: Use Task Manager when Activity Monitor shows load from cmux but does not identify the responsible workspace, agent, or browser pane.
taskManager.blogCta: Read the <link>Task Manager feature story</link> for the workflow and launch context.

testimonials.mitchellh: Another day another libghostty-based project, this time a macOS terminal with vertical tabs, better organization/notifications, embedded/scriptable browser specifically targeted towards people who use a ton of terminal-based agentic workflows.
testimonials.steipete: I'm late to the party, but cmux is great. Current split: Codex Mac app for knowledge work, learning, reading; cmux + Codex CLI for coding.
testimonials.schrockn: This is exactly the product I've been looking for. After two hours this am I've in love.
testimonials.egrefen: I've been using this all weekend and it's amazing.
testimonials.max4c: this has been my favorite tool for past two weeks
testimonials.lucas_flatwhite: It's not too late. I use Ghostty a lot, and cmux is love too. Vertical tabs, notification rings, built-in browser, split panes, GPU acceleration... there are so many real benefits!
testimonials.yamadashy: I've used cmux for a while and I like it. It feels less like 'this is amazing' and more like 'yes, this is it.' It also pairs well with k1Low/mo.
testimonials.minixalpha: I also use cmux as my main terminal and recommend it to coworkers. The notifications, workspaces, and shortcuts improve efficiency when running multiple jobs in parallel. Even though cmux is a bit ugly, its functionality makes it indispensable.
testimonials.juan_barbat: I had an issue with the browser, but cmux is indispensable in my day to day.
testimonials.yousefrol: My suggestion is just use cmux. It solved almost every terminal problem for me.
testimonials.tobiasgloeckler: I just installed cmux. I had been using Ghostty, but cmux is even better suited for AI agents and coding.
testimonials.wescld: Man, I don't know how I lived so long without cmux.
testimonials.jinchenma_ai: cmux is the terminal tool I use most lately. It's open source and free. It has basically replaced iTerm2 for me and perfectly solves the multi-terminal window layout problem.
testimonials.zvasil: I moved to it from Warp a while ago and it felt like switching to a rocket. It's native macOS Swift and being developed super actively.
testimonials.immazzystar: A tool I've liked using recently: cmux. I no longer have to switch terminal windows constantly.
testimonials.blitz_zidan: cmux is my favorite terminal lately. Personally, I think it's really good for running multiple terminals.
testimonials.asaza: cmux looks so good it might finally be time to say goodbye to VSCode
testimonials.ssarisen: If you need to work with multiple terminals, you should definitely try cmux. When a terminal waits for your input, it sends an automatic notification.
testimonials.johnthedebs: Hey, this looks seriously awesome. Love the ideas here, specifically: the programmability, layered UI, browser w/ api. Looking forward to giving this a spin. Also want to add that I really appreciate Mitchell Hashimoto creating libghostty; it feels like an exciting time to be a terminal user.
testimonials.joeriddles: Vertical tabs in my terminal! I never thought of that before. I use and love Firefox vertical tabs.
testimonials.dchu17: Gave this a run and it was pretty intuitive. Good work!
testimonials.afruth: I like it, ran it in the past day on three parallel projects each with several worktrees. Having this paired with lazygit and yazi / nvim made me a bit more productive than usual without having to chase multiple ghostty / iTerm instances. Also feels more natural than tmux.
testimonials.northprint: Tried cmux since it looked good — it's good
testimonials.scottw: This has been such a useful find. I can't recommend it enough.
testimonials.johnblythe: grabbed this over the weekend and loved it. been waiting for something like this.
testimonials.bchris91: This is exactly what I've wanted. Amazing job thank you!
testimonials.connorelsea: Been using this for a week and it's fantastic. Vert tab for each WIP task. Inside, claudes on one side and browser with PR and resources on the other, switch between tasks and stay organized. Mix that with skills to have Claude watch CI recursively, etc. feeling enlightened tbh

bestTerminal.metaTitle: The best terminal for Mac in 2026 — cmux
bestTerminal.metaDescription: An honest comparison of the best macOS terminals: cmux, Ghostty, iTerm2, Warp, Terminal.app, Alacritty, kitty, WezTerm, and tmux. Pick by workflow. cmux is built for multitasking, organization, and programmability.
bestTerminal.title: The best terminal for Mac
bestTerminal.intro: There is no single best terminal, only the best one for how you work. Below is an honest comparison of the strong macOS options. We build cmux, so we will be upfront: cmux is built for multitasking, organization, and programmability, and we will say where the others are the better call.
bestTerminal.warpBuiltFor: Built-in AI and a blocks UI
bestTerminal.weztermBuiltFor: GPU terminal with a built-in multiplexer
bestTerminal.cmuxBody: cmux is a native macOS terminal built on Ghostty's terminal engine, libghostty. That means the terminal grid inherits Ghostty's strengths: fast GPU rendering, excellent font and color rendering, modern terminal compatibility, and your existing Ghostty themes, fonts, colors, and cursor settings. cmux adds the layer Ghostty intentionally does not try to be: vertical workspaces, split panes, notification rings, git/port/agent context in the sidebar, a scriptable CLI and Unix socket, a programmable browser, and an iOS companion. Pick cmux when you want Ghostty-quality terminal rendering plus organization and automation for many tasks or AI coding agents.
bestTerminal.ghosttyBody: Ghostty is the fast, native terminal whose engine, libghostty, powers cmux. Compared with older CPU-rendered terminals and heavier Electron-style terminals, Ghostty's strengths are low-latency GPU rendering, crisp font and color handling, strong terminal compatibility, simple file-based configuration, and no cloud account requirement. If you want one clean terminal window with those rendering advantages and no workspace or automation layer, Ghostty is excellent on its own. If you want those same Ghostty rendering advantages plus workspaces, notifications, scripting, and agent orchestration, use cmux.
bestTerminal.iterm2Body: The mature, endlessly configurable macOS terminal. Deep profiles, triggers, and tmux integration. The safe default if you want maximum features and are not orchestrating many tasks at once.
bestTerminal.warpBody: A Rust terminal with a built-in AI assistant and a blocks command UI, behind an account, and available beyond macOS. A good fit if you want AI baked into the terminal itself or need Linux and Windows too.
bestTerminal.terminalAppBody: The built-in macOS terminal. Always there, zero setup. Fine for light use; most power users eventually want more.
bestTerminal.otherBody: Fast GPU terminals for people who like configuring everything in a file. Alacritty is deliberately minimal, kitty is feature-rich and scriptable, and WezTerm bundles its own multiplexer. All are cross-platform and a good fit if a single, highly tuned terminal is what you want.
bestTerminal.tmuxBody: Not a terminal but a multiplexer you run inside one. Unbeatable for persistent sessions over SSH on remote servers. cmux can attach to remote tmux sessions when you need that.

ghostty.metaTitle: cmux is built on Ghostty (libghostty) — cmux
ghostty.metaDescription: cmux uses libghostty, the engine behind Ghostty, for GPU-accelerated terminal rendering, then adds workspaces, vertical tabs, notifications, an iOS companion, and a socket API for multitasking and AI coding agents.
ghostty.title: cmux is built on Ghostty
ghostty.intro: cmux is not a fork of Ghostty. It embeds <link>libghostty</link>, the library at the core of the Ghostty terminal, for GPU-accelerated rendering, the same way an app uses WebKit for web views. Ghostty is a standalone terminal; cmux is a different application built on top of its rendering engine.
ghostty.addsIntro: libghostty gives cmux a fast, accurate terminal. cmux builds an application around it for multitasking, organization, and programmability:
ghostty.add1: Workspaces in a vertical sidebar, each showing its git branch, working directory, ports, and the latest line of agent output.
ghostty.add2: Notification rings when a pane needs your attention, plus an iOS companion app to check on your terminals from your phone.
ghostty.add3: Vertical tabs and split panes that scale to dozens of sessions.
ghostty.add4: A CLI and Unix socket API to script workspaces, panes, input, and an in-app browser.
ghostty.whyBody: Reusing libghostty means cmux inherits Ghostty's rendering quality and performance instead of reimplementing a terminal, and stays focused on the workspace, organization, and automation layer that sits above the terminal grid. Your existing ~/.config/ghostty/config for themes, fonts, and colors is read directly.

claude.metaTitle: A terminal for Claude Code — cmux
claude.metaDescription: cmux is a native macOS terminal built for running Claude Code: workspaces that organize parallel agents, notification rings when Claude needs you, Claude Code teams as native panes, an iOS companion, and a scriptable CLI.
claude.title: A terminal for Claude Code
claude.intro: cmux is a native macOS terminal built for running AI coding agents, and Claude Code is a first-class fit. cmux is just a terminal, so claude runs in any workspace out of the box, and the things that make running agents painful, keeping track of many at once and noticing when they need you, are what cmux is built for.
claude.organizeTitle: Run many Claude Code sessions, organized
claude.organizeBody: Open a workspace per task and run Claude Code in each. The vertical sidebar shows every workspace with its git branch, directory, ports, and the latest line of Claude's output, so a dozen parallel sessions stay legible instead of buried in tabs.
claude.notifyTitle: Notification rings when Claude needs you
claude.notifyBody: When Claude Code finishes or asks for input, the pane rings and the sidebar shows an unread badge, so you can let several agents run and come back to the one that needs a decision. Notifications fire automatically, and you can also trigger them from Claude Code hooks.
claude.teamsTitle: Claude Code teams as native panes
claude.teamsBody: cmux runs Claude Code's teammate mode with one command, and the teammates spawn as native cmux splits with their own sidebar metadata and notifications, no tmux required. See the <link>Claude Code teams docs</link>.
claude.iosTitle: Check on Claude from your phone
claude.iosBody: cmux has an iOS companion app (beta): pair your iPhone with your Mac and check on your Claude Code sessions, with optional notification forwarding, while you are away from the desk.
claude.scriptBody: Every action is available through the cmux CLI and a Unix socket: create a workspace, launch Claude Code in it, send input, read the screen, and drive an in-app browser to verify changes, all from a script.
claude.faqQ1: Does Claude Code work in cmux?
claude.faqA1: Yes. cmux is a standard macOS terminal, so claude runs in any workspace with no extra setup.
claude.faqQ2: Can I run Claude Code teams in cmux?
claude.faqA2: Yes. cmux runs Claude Code's teammate mode with one command and teammates spawn as native cmux splits with their own sidebar metadata and notifications, no tmux required.
claude.faqQ3: How do I know when Claude needs input?
claude.faqA3: The pane rings and the sidebar shows an unread badge when Claude Code finishes or asks for input, so you can let several agents run and come back to the one that needs you.
claude.faqQ4: Is cmux free to use with Claude Code?
claude.faqA4: Yes. cmux is free and open source for macOS.

codex.metaTitle: A terminal for Codex CLI — cmux
codex.metaDescription: cmux is a native macOS terminal built for running OpenAI Codex CLI: workspaces that organize parallel agents, notification rings, oh-my-codex, an iOS companion, and a scriptable CLI and socket API.
codex.title: A terminal for Codex CLI
codex.intro: cmux is a native macOS terminal built for AI coding agents, and the OpenAI Codex CLI runs in it out of the box. cmux is just a terminal, so codex works in any workspace, with cmux adding the multitasking, organization, and programmability around it.
codex.organizeBody: Run Codex in its own workspace per task. The vertical sidebar shows each one with its git branch, directory, ports, and latest output, so several parallel Codex runs stay organized instead of lost in tabs.
codex.notifyTitle: Notification rings when Codex needs you
codex.notifyBody: When Codex finishes or asks for input, the pane rings and the sidebar flags it unread, so you can run several at once and return to the one that needs attention. Notifications fire automatically and can also be driven from agent hooks.
codex.omoBody: cmux ships an oh-my-codex integration that runs Codex in a cmux-aware environment so its activity surfaces as native cmux panes. See the oh-my-codex docs.
codex.iosTitle: Check on Codex from your phone
codex.iosBody: cmux has an iOS companion app (beta): pair your iPhone with your Mac to check on your Codex runs, with optional notification forwarding, while you are away from the desk.
codex.scriptBody: Everything is available through the cmux CLI and a Unix socket: create a workspace, launch Codex, send input, read the screen, take screenshots, and drive an in-app browser, all from a script.
codex.faqQ1: Does Codex CLI work in cmux?
codex.faqA1: Yes. cmux is a standard macOS terminal, so codex runs in any workspace with no extra setup.
codex.faqQ2: Can I run Codex next to other agents?
codex.faqA2: Yes. Open a workspace per task and run Codex, Claude Code, or OpenCode side by side. The sidebar keeps every session legible.
codex.faqQ3: How do I know when Codex needs input?
codex.faqA3: The pane rings and the sidebar shows an unread badge when Codex finishes or asks for input, so you can let it run and come back when it needs you.
codex.faqQ4: Is cmux free to use with Codex?
codex.faqA4: Yes. cmux is free and open source for macOS.

opencode.metaDescription: cmux is a native macOS terminal built for running OpenCode: workspaces that organize parallel agents, notification rings, oh-my-opencode multi-model orchestration, an iOS companion, and a scriptable CLI.
opencode.title: A terminal for OpenCode
opencode.intro: cmux is a native macOS terminal built for AI coding agents, and OpenCode runs in it out of the box. cmux is just a terminal, so opencode works in any workspace, with cmux providing the multitasking, organization, and programmability around it.
opencode.organizeBody: Run OpenCode per task in its own workspace. The vertical sidebar shows each with its git branch, directory, ports, and latest output, so parallel runs stay organized.
opencode.notifyBody: When OpenCode needs you, the pane rings and the sidebar marks it unread, so you can run several at once and come back to the one waiting on a decision.
opencode.omoBody: cmux ships cmux omo, which runs OpenCode with the oh-my-openagent plugin so multiple models (Claude, GPT, Gemini, Grok) orchestrate as parallel agents, and each spawned agent becomes a native cmux split. See the oh-my-opencode docs.
opencode.iosTitle: Check on OpenCode from your phone
opencode.iosBody: cmux has an iOS companion app (beta): pair your iPhone with your Mac to check on your OpenCode runs, with optional notification forwarding, while you are away from the desk.
opencode.scriptBody: Every action is available through the cmux CLI and a Unix socket, so you can create workspaces, launch OpenCode, send input, read the screen, and drive an in-app browser from a script.
opencode.faqA1: Yes. cmux is a standard macOS terminal, so opencode runs in any workspace with no extra setup.
opencode.faqQ2: Can I run OpenCode next to other agents?
opencode.faqA2: Yes. Open a workspace per task and run OpenCode, Claude Code, or Codex side by side. The sidebar keeps every session legible.
opencode.faqQ3: How do I know when OpenCode needs input?
opencode.faqA3: The pane rings and the sidebar shows an unread badge when OpenCode finishes or asks for input, so you can let it run and come back when it needs you.
opencode.faqQ4: Is cmux free to use with OpenCode?
opencode.faqA4: Yes. cmux is free and open source for macOS.

sections.title: Why cmux is different
sections.body: cmux starts from the terminal instead of the agent. Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode, Gemini CLI, Aider, Amp, and Cursor CLI all run normally, while cmux adds the missing control plane around them: workspaces, split panes, unread state, notification rings, and a socket API.

sections.title: Multitasking and organization
sections.body: Agent-heavy work breaks down when every session becomes another tab with a vague title. cmux keeps work in vertical workspaces that show branch, directory, ports, and the latest notification text, so several agents stay legible while they run.

sections.title: Performance under load
sections.body: cmux is a native Swift/AppKit app built on libghostty, not Electron. That gives it a native Mac UI and Ghostty-based terminal rendering while keeping the orchestration layer separate from the agents, test runners, browsers, and dev servers you run inside it.

sections.title: Keyboard-first supervision
sections.body: cmux treats attention as a first-class workflow. Cmd+Shift+U jumps to the latest unread pane, and Cmd+Control+U cycles unread work without clearing it. Those shortcuts are built for people running enough agents that manual tab scanning stops working.

sections.title: Worktree support is a primitive, not a product box
sections.body: Built-in worktree support can be too narrow for teams with split frontend/backend repos, setup scripts, remote machines, no-git projects, or code-to-main flows. cmux stays programmable so Claude Code, Codex, custom commands, and repo scripts can create the workspace shape your codebase needs.

bestTerminalForAgents.faqQ1: Is cmux an AI coding agent?

bestTerminalForAgents.faqA2: Any CLI agent works, including Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode, Gemini CLI, Aider, Amp, and Cursor CLI.
bestTerminalForAgents.faqQ3: Why not just use terminal tabs?
bestTerminalForAgents.faqA3: Tabs work for a few shells. cmux adds workspace metadata, notification rings, unread navigation, split panes, browser automation, and a scriptable socket for agent-heavy workflows.

sections.title: Automation reaches outside the shell
sections.body: cmux exposes app-level actions through a CLI and Unix socket, including browser automation. Agents can inspect web pages, click, type, read terminal screens, and take screenshots from the same workspace.

cmuxVsGhostty.faqQ1: Is cmux built by the Ghostty team?
cmuxVsGhostty.faqA1: No. cmux is a separate project that uses libghostty for terminal rendering.
cmuxVsGhostty.faqA2: Yes. cmux reads your Ghostty config for terminal rendering choices such as fonts, themes, colors, and cursor behavior.
cmuxVsGhostty.faqQ3: When should I use cmux instead of Ghostty?
cmuxVsGhostty.faqA3: Use cmux when you run multiple coding agents and want workspaces, notification rings, unread navigation, browser panes, and programmability around them.

cta.freeOpenSource: cmux is free and open source for macOS.

agents.metaTitle: A terminal for coding agents — cmux
agents.metaDescription: cmux is a native macOS terminal built for running coding agents: Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode, Gemini CLI, Aider, Amp, and Cursor. Workspaces for parallel agents, notification rings, vertical tabs, an iOS companion, and a scriptable CLI.
agents.title: A terminal for coding agents
agents.intro: cmux is a native macOS terminal built on Ghostty for running AI coding agents. It is just a terminal, so any agent CLI runs out of the box, and the things that make running agents painful, keeping track of many at once and noticing when they need you, are what cmux is built for.
agents.agentsBody: cmux works with every agent CLI. Each page below covers how cmux helps you run that agent: parallel workspaces, notification rings, vertical tabs, an iOS companion, and a scriptable socket.
agents.organizeBody: Open a workspace per task and run any agent in each. The vertical sidebar shows every workspace with its git branch, directory, ports, and the latest line of output, so a dozen parallel agents stay legible instead of buried in tabs.
agents.notifyTitle: Notification rings when an agent needs you
agents.notifyBody: When an agent finishes or asks for input, the pane rings and the sidebar shows an unread badge, so you can let several agents run and come back to the one that needs a decision. Notifications fire automatically and you can also trigger them from agent hooks.
agents.scriptBody: Every action is available through the cmux CLI and a Unix socket: create a workspace, launch an agent in it, send input, read the screen, and drive an in-app browser to verify changes, all from a script.
agents.faqQ1: Which coding agents work in cmux?
agents.faqA1: cmux is a standard macOS terminal, so any CLI agent works: Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode, Gemini CLI, Aider, Amp, Cursor, and anything else you run from a shell.
agents.faqQ2: Do I have to configure each agent?
agents.faqA2: No. Agents run exactly as they do in any terminal. cmux adds workspaces, notification rings, and a scriptable socket on top without changing how the agent itself runs.
agents.faqQ3: Can I run several agents at once?
agents.faqA3: Yes. Open a workspace per task, run a different agent in each, and the sidebar keeps every session legible with its branch, directory, and latest output line.
agents.faqA4: Yes. cmux is free and open source for macOS.
